text:
Perhaps the most classic example of this fallacy is the argument for the existence of God.  Due to the understanding of nature through science, many of the arguments that used to be used for God (or gods) were abandoned, only to be replaced with new ones, usually involving questions to which science has not definitively answered yet.  The move from creationism to intelligent design is a prime example.  Currently the origin of life is a popular argument for God (although a classic argument from ignorance), and an area where we very well may have a scientific answer in the next decade, at which time, the “origin of life” argument will fade away and be replaced by another, thus moving the figurative goalposts farther back as our understanding of the natural world increases.Exception: This fallacy should not be confused with an argument or set of arguments, with multiple propositions inherent in the argument.  The reason for the difference between this kind of argument and the moving the goalposts fallacy, is a subtle one, but indicated by a strong initial claim (“has to be”, “must”, “required for”, etc.) that gets answered and/or what appears to be ad hoc objections that follow eventually leading to an impossible request for proof.
Fun Fact: The name “moving the goalposts” comes from the analogy of kicking a perfect field goal in American football, only to have the goalposts be moved on you. This would be very unfair.

This a logical fallacy frequently used on the Internet. No academic sources could be found.

text: I don't see how you can believe he is not guilty of that crime. He's a terrible person and I can prove it. In fact, several years ago he was convicted of that same kind of crime.
raw_response: {"label": "fallacy of extension", "reason": "The argument assumes that because someone has been convicted of a similar crime in the past, they must be guilty of the current crime, without considering other evidence or potential mitigating factors."}

text: My opponent is trying to convince you that we evolved from monkeys who were swinging from trees; a truly ludicrous claim.
raw_response: {"label": "ad populum", "reason": "The argument attempts to discredit the opponent's claim by suggesting it's a ludicrous claim, appealing to the audience's belief that evolution is false."}

text: In the race for mayor, Lara says a vote for her opponent will lead to you losing your job. What fallacy has she committed?
raw_response: {"label": "fallacy of extension", "reason": "The argument assumes a direct causal link between a vote for the opponent and job loss, without establishing a necessary or sufficient connection."}

text: Historians agree that studying the past is the best way to avoid repeating mistakes.
raw_response: {"label": "equivocation", "reason": "The statement asserts that studying the past is the 'best way' to avoid mistakes, which is a veiled reference to a specific set of mistakes. It doesn't explicitly state *what* mistakes are being avoided, making it a form of equivocation."}

text: This type of ad taps into a person's desire to be part of a group.
raw_response: {"label": "ad populum", "reason": "The statement implies that the argument is appealing to a group's desire to belong, which is a characteristic of ad populum."}

text: Four out of five dentists recommend Happy Glossy Smiley toothpaste brand. Therefore, it must be great.
raw_response: {"label": "fallacy of extension", "reason": "The argument assumes that because a few people like a product, it must be good without providing any evidence or reasoning beyond the initial observation."}

text: If I go to the bathroom, I will miss the notes, then I'll do poorly on a test, then I won't get into college, so I won't go to the bathroom.
raw_response: {"label": "circular reasoning", "reason": "The conclusion is used as a premise to support the conclusion."}

text: One cigarette will lead youth to addiction
raw_response: {"label": "fallacy of extension", "reason": "The argument assumes a direct causal link between smoking and addiction without sufficient evidence or justification."}

text: If the government allows psychologists to prescribe medications there will be no basis to block them from obtaining competence and legal authority to conduct other traditionally medical procedures such as diagnosing minor skin irritations, treating a sprained ankle, setting a broken bone, and performing neurosurgery." Or: "Never reduce a fee for any patient for any reason or else you'll find yourself constantly reducing fees for everyone, everyone will take advantage of you, your patients will lose respect for you and for therapy, and you'll loose money and go bankrupt.
